summary: This satirical post mocks Java's excessive use of access modifiers (private, final, protected) and deprecation annotations by imagining Wikileaks 'liberating' open source Java code by removing these restrictions. Through fake interviews with fictional developers, Yegge argues that Java's encapsulation obsession contradicts the spirit of open source software. The piece contrasts Java's restrictive philosophy with Python's more permissive 'we're all adults here' approach. Ultimately, it's a critique of how Java culture prioritizes control over usability, even when the code is supposedly 'open.'
key_points: Java's private/final/protected modifiers often prevent legitimate use cases, even in open source code where users theoretically have full access
The @deprecated pattern in Java creates busywork where developers deprecate code just to force users onto newer, more complicated APIs
Python's philosophy treats users as adults who can make their own decisions, while Java's philosophy assumes users need protection from themselves
The Eclipse ecosystem exemplifies the worst of Java's over-engineering tendencies with pre-deprecated code and framework lock-in
Open source Java is philosophically contradictory: 'here's my code, but fuck you, use it exactly how I tell you'
money_quotes: Open Source does *not* mean the source is somehow 'open'.
If I make something private, it means that no matter how desperately you need to call it, I should be able to prevent you from doing so, even long after I've gone to the grave.
Why can't users of my Open Source Java library simply shake their fists and curse my family name with their dying breaths?
If people could keep using the older, more convenient APIs I made for them, then why the fuck would they use my newer, ridiculously complicated ones?
We have a policy of releasing entire Java packages in which every single class, interface and method is deprecated right out of the box, starting at version 1.0.
When Python developers release Open Source code, they are saying: Here, I worked hard on this. I hope you like it. Use it however you think best.
Whereas Java developers who release Open Source code are saying: Here, I worked hard on this. I hope you like it. But use it exactly how I tell you to use it, because fuck you, it's my code.
key_insight: Java's obsession with access control and deprecation reflects a paternalistic philosophy that fundamentally contradicts the collaborative spirit of open source software.
